PREFACE
The introduction of the competence-based curriculum (CBC) marks a transformative step in 
improving education quality and relevance. This Form 1 Geography Teaching Module has been 
meticulously designed to support teachers to effectively deliver Geography lessons under the new 
curriculum. The module aims to bridge the gap created by the absence of approved textbooks and 
to equip educators with practical tools, activities, and assessments tailored to the learners‚Äô level 
and needs. As a Ministry, we understand the challenges that come with transitioning to a new 
curriculum. This module reflects our commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible 
for teachers, by providing structured guidance, suggested activities, and assessments that align 
with the CBC. However, we also encourage educators to supplement this module with their own 
research and innovations to address any gaps that may arise during implementation. The suggested 
activities and assessments provided in this module are designed to foster practical and critical 
thinking skills in learners while ensuring relevance and adaptability to different school contexts. 
Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom dynamics and to 
explore alternative acceptable approaches when necessary. This flexibility is key to ensuring that 
all learners, regardless of their environment, benefit from meaningful and impactful Geography 
education. We hope this module will serve as a valuable resource for teachers as they navigate 
the new Competence-Based Curriculum, enhancing their ability to inspire and equip learners 
with essential Geography knowledge, skills and values.
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

This Geography Teaching Module is designed to provide the teacher with a scope and sequence 
of topics considered necessary to be offered at Ordinary Secondary School Level in Form 1 
of term II. This is with a view to provide guidance to the teaching and learning of this unique, 
but yet exiting blend of concepts in Geography for teachers and other experts in the field to 
appropriately offer relevant lessons at Ordinary Secondary Level.
Many thanks go to individuals, institutions and organisations that participated in the successful 
development of this module. These include; teachers, lecturers from colleges, public universities 
in Zambia and in particular, the University of Zambia.
Sincere gratitude also goes to the Zambia Education Enhancement Project (ZEEP) for the 
financial support and Zambia Educational Publishing House (ZEPH) for the technical support 
towards the development and finalisation of the module.
Last but not the least, the commitment and hard work of all the staff at the Curriculum Development 
Centre in ensuring that this module comes to reality is recognised.
Charles Ndakala (Dr.)
Director ‚Äď Directorate of Curriculum Development

INTRODUCTION
This Geography Teaching Module provides guidance to the teacher on how to deliver competence-
based lessons on the fundamental concepts of Geography. This module aims to help a teacher 
develop an understanding of the indoor and outdoor environment required to prepare lessons 
which help learners develop critical thinking, problem-solving, and analytical skills. This module 
is committed to providing an enriching and supportive educational environment where teachers 
can prepare and deliver lessons which stimulate interest in Geography for learners. This module 
seeks to support teachers to prepare lessons for both academic performance and their future roles 
as knowledgeable and responsible citizens in a scientifically advanced society by encouraging 
inquiry, curiosity, and a love of the subject. 
It is expected that this Geography teaching module, once implemented successfully, will inspire 
and empower learners to achieve their full potential, equipping them with the knowledge and 
skills necessary to navigate and contribute to the world around them. This module is designed to 
ensure learners develop a deep understanding of Geography principles while also acquiring the 
practical skills and competencies needed for further education and careers in Geography.
How to use the Module
To effectively use this Geography Teaching Module for Form 1:
	
¬Č Read and familiarise yourself with the module‚Äôs content, learning activities, and 
assessment guides.
	
¬Č Plan your lessons in advance using the module‚Äôs suggested teaching and learning 
activities.
	
¬Č Use a variety of teaching methods including demonstrations, discussions, group 
work, and hands-on experiments.
	
¬Č Encourage active learning by asking open-ended questions, promoting critical 
thinking, and fostering problem-solving skills.
	
¬Č Assess learners learning regularly using the module‚Äôs suggested assessment strategies 
and tools.
	
¬Č Provide feedback and support to learners, helping them to identify areas for 
improvement and develop their skills.
	
¬Č Integrate technology into your teaching using multimedia resources and interactive 
simulations to enhance student engagement and understanding.
	
¬Č Monitor student progress and adjust your teaching strategies as needed, to ensure that 
all students meet the learning objectives
By following these steps, you can effectively use this Geography Teaching Module for Form 1 
to support your teaching and promote learning.
Suggested Teaching and Learning Materials
The study of Geography content requires hands-on experiences, visual aids, and interactive re¬≠
sources to strengthen deep understanding and appreciation. To support the teaching and learning 
of Geography in Form 1, the suggested teaching and learning materials are meant to:

¬Č Enhance learner engagement and motivation in Geography
	
¬Č Develop practical skills and techniques in Geography
	
¬Č Promote critical thinking, problem-solving, and analytical skills
	
¬Č Support differentiated instruction and inclusive learning
By utilising these suggested teaching and learning materials, teachers can create a dynamic and 
supportive learning environment that promotes academic excellence, creativity, and scientific 
literacy in Geography for Form 1 learners.
Learning Environment Set Up
Creating an effective learning environment for teaching and learning Geography is important in 
deepening understanding and application of concepts in real life context. The learning environ¬≠
ment set-up aims to create a safe, inclusive, and engaging space.
	
¬Č Indoor learning set up: Conducted in a classroom with controlled environment 
including seating arrangements, lighting and temperature control.
	
¬Č Outdoor learning set up: Takes place in open spaces such as school surroundings, 
gardens, or natural environments
Teaching Methodology
The effective teaching methodologies in Geography include:
	
¬Č Conducting experiments: Demonstrate key principles and encourage curiosity among 
learners.
	
¬Č Collaborative learning: Pair learners to work together, promoting peer-to-peer 
teaching, discussion, and problem-solving.
	
¬Č Conceptual learning: Connect Geography concepts to everyday life, industry, or 
current events, making learning relevant and meaningful.
	
¬Č Differentiated instructions: Tailor teaching to meet diverse learning styles, abilities, 
and interests of different learners.
	
¬Č Feedback and Reflection: Encourage learners to reflect on their learning, providing 
constructive feedback to guide improvement.
	
¬Č Inquiry-based learning: Encourage learners to explore, investigate, and discover 
Geography concepts through hands-on experiments and activities.
	
¬Č Integration of Technology: Use digital tools, simulations, and visualisations to 
enhance engagement, understanding, and analysis.
	
¬Č Problem-based learning: Present real-world problems or case studies, requiring 
students to apply Geography principles to develop solutions.
	
¬Č Project-based learning: Assign open-ended projects, allowing students to design, 
conduct, and present research or applications of Geography concepts.
By implementing these methodologies, a teacher can create an engaging, inclusive, and effective 
learning environment.

Time Allocation
The standard minimum learner-teacher contact time for Geography at Secondary School Level 
is 3hours 20 minutes per week, translating into five (5) periods. The duration for a single period 
is 40 minutes. The contact time at Secondary School Level is planned in such a way as to give 
ample time for practical activities.

1.1.	 TOPIC 1: NATURAL RESOURCES
Introduction
Natural resources are materials like water, air, trees, and minerals that come from nature and are 
essential for life. Some of these such as sunlight and forests, can be replaced over time, while 
others, such as oil and minerals, take millions of years to form and could run out.
Responsible use of natural resources is important to ensure they last. This includes avoiding 
waste, protecting the environment, and using resources wisely. As Zambia‚Äôs population grows, 
more resources are needed for food, energy, and development. This creates pressure on natural 
resources, so careful management is necessary to balance today‚Äôs needs with future sustainability.
Managing natural resources involves actions like replanting trees, protecting water sources, and 
reducing pollution. By doing this, we can support both the people of today and future generations 
while keeping the environment healthy.
This topic will focus on exploring responsible use of natural resources, the relationship 
between population and these resources and sustainable management practices that ensure their 
preservation for future generations while meeting the needs of the present. 
Hook: ‚ÄėHave you wondered where your desk, clothes and the food you eat (sandwich, 
nshima, bread) comes from? The answer lies in something we often take for granted: 
natural resources‚Äô
  
General Competences
The general competencies in this topic are: Analytical thinking, Citizenship, Collaboration, and 
Environmental Sustainability.
Key Terms:
Key terms
	
¬Č Natural Resources
	
¬Č Preservation
	
¬Č Sustainable management
	
¬Č Practices 
	
¬Č Carrying Capacity
SUB-TOPIC 1.1.1: NATURAL RESOURCES
Natural resources are things found in nature that people use to live and grow. They include:
Renewable resources such as sunlight, water, and trees, which can be replaced naturally over 
time and non-renewable resources such as oil, coal, and minerals, which take a long time 
to form and can run out. These resources help us build homes, make electricity, grow food, 
and more. Managing them carefully ensures they last for future generations. This sub-topic will 
explore responsible use of natural resources.
Specific Competence (s) ;
Learners to -: 
Use renewable and non-renewable resources responsibly
 
Activity 1
Identifying different natural resources

Teaching Module

Learning Environment
The learning activity would be outdoor or indoor set up.
Indoor Set up
	
¬Č Divide learners into smaller groups based on their strengths to foster collaboration.
	
¬Č Ask each group to brainstorm and list environmental or natural resources they are 
familiar with.
	
¬Č Provide charts, pictures, or physical representations of natural resources to enhance 
visual learning.
	
¬Č Encourage discussion within the groups guiding them to explore examples such as 
water, forests, air, soil, minerals and wildlife
Outdoor Set up:
	
¬Č Before heading outdoors, discuss with learners what natural resources are and 
provide examples of both living (e.g., plants, animals) and non-living (e.g., rocks, 
soil, water) resources.
Observation Walk:
	
¬Č Take the learners to an outdoor area, such as a school surrounding, nearby park, or 
community space.
	
¬Č Ask them to observe their surroundings and identify as many natural resources as 
possible in the environment.
Categorization:
	
¬Č Divide learners into small groups and have each group categorize the resources 
they identified into living and non-living.
	
¬Č Encourage discussions within the groups to refine their understanding.
 Presentations:
	
¬Č Let each group share their findings with the class in a brief presentation. They can 
use sketches, diagrams, or other creative methods to illustrate their observations.
Content Tips
	
¬Č Natural resources are the materials and substances that occur naturally within the 
environment and are used by humans to fulfill their needs and wants. 
	
¬Č There are two types of natural resources and these are renewable and non-renewable 
resources. 
	
¬Č These resources are essential for sustaining life and supporting economic activities.
	
¬Č Identifying natural resources involves understanding the types of resources 
available in nature and the methods used to locate, classify, and evaluate them. 
	
¬Č They include; 
ÔÉľ Water resources such as Rivers, lakes, groundwater, rainwater, etc.
ÔÉľ Forests resources such as trees, medicinal plants, bamboos.
ÔÉľ Energy resources such as solar, wind, hydro, geothermal, biomass, fossil fuels.
ÔÉľ Soil and agricultural resources
ÔÉľ Fossil fuels (Coal, Oil, Natural Gas)
ÔÉľ Minerals and metals such as copper, gold, 
	
¬Č Methods of identifying natural resources include the following
ÔÉľ Geological surveys
ÔÉľ Remote sensing

ÔÉľ Exploratory drilling
ÔÉľ Sampling and analysis
ÔÉľ Field surveys
ÔÉľ Lab analysis confirms composition and quality of resources.
	
¬Č Importance of identifying natural resources 
ÔÉľ Economic benefits; through extraction process and trade.
ÔÉľ Sustainable management; understanding the distribution, accessibility and 
quality of natural resources can inform sustainability management practices. 
ÔÉľ Environmental conservation; it helps to mitigate the environmental impacts of 
the extraction and use. 
	
¬Č Challenges in identifying natural resources
ÔÉľ Technological limitations; such as accessing remote or hard-to-reach areas. 
ÔÉľ Lack of knowledge
ÔÉľ Social and economic factors; such as local communities, government policies, 
and market demand. 
Teaching and Learning Materials 
	
¬Č Note books for outdoor activities.
	
¬Č Pictures and videos of Natural resources
	
¬Č Samples of some natural resources
Assessment
	
¬Č Learners through discussions, debates demonstrate how some resources could be 
depleted.
	
¬Č Learners explain the dangers of depleting the natural resources.
	
¬Č Learners display posters on how to avoid depletion of natural resources.
	
¬Č Learners plant trees as a means of avoiding forestry depletion
Activity 2
 
Describing the types of natural resources: (Renewable - water, land, wildlife, solar‚Ä¶ 
and non-renewable - minerals, fossil fuels...)
Learning Environment
The learning activity would be outdoor or indoor set up.
Indoor Set up:
	
¬Č Divide learners into smaller groups, ensuring diversity within each group to 
encourage collaboration and varied perspectives.
	
¬Č Check learner understanding of concepts of renewable and non-renewable 
resources. Provide clear definitions and examples:
Ôāß Renewable resources: Those that replenish naturally and can be used 
repeatedly (e.g., sunlight, wind, water).
Ôāß Non-renewable resources: Those that are finite and cannot be replaced after 
use (e.g., fossil fuels, minerals).
	
¬Č Have each group discuss and classify the listed resources into renewable and non-
renewable categories.
	
¬Č Encourage learners to justify their categorization by explaining their reasoning.
	
¬Č Let each group present their findings creatively, such as:

Ôāß Drawing posters showing renewable and non-renewable resources.
Ôāß Preparing short skits or role-plays illustrating the usage of these resources.
	
¬Č After presentations, hold a group discussion to emphasise:
Ôāß Why conserving non-renewable resources is important.
Ôāß How renewable resources can be maximized.
Outdoor Set up:
	
¬Č Ask learners to observe and describe the natural resources found in their immediate 
environment.
	
¬Č Learners to compile their observations of natural resources and categorise them into 
renewable and non-renewable describe Renewable and non- renewable resources.
Content Tips
	
¬Č Renewable resources are natural resources that can replenish or regenerate over 
time through natural processes or sustainable management practices.
	
¬Č These resources are typically derived from the Earth‚Äôs natural cycles, such as 
the water cycle, carbon cycle and photosynthesis, ensuring they are available for 
continuous use if managed responsibly.
	
¬Č Examples of renewable resources include sunlight, wind, water and forests.
	
¬Č Renewable resources can be continuously reproduced or regenerated, ensuring 
their availability for future generations if managed sustainably.
	
¬Č Non-renewable resources are those that form over a long period of time and 
cannot be replaced once depleted.
	
¬Č Examples include fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) and minerals (gold, copper, 
iron), etc.
	
¬Č Non-renewable resources are finite and their extraction and use often lead to 
environmental degradation and resource depletion.
Teaching and Learning Materials
	
¬Č Note books for outdoor activities.
	
¬Č Pictures and videos of Natural resources
	
¬Č Samples of some natural resources
Assessment
	
¬Č Learners explain the importance of renewable and non-renewable resources

Activity 3
 Utilizing Renewable and Non-renewable Resources responsibly
Learning Environment
	
¬Č The learning activity would be indoor or indoor set up.
Context Setting 
	
¬Č Arrange desks in small groups of 4‚Äď6 learners to encourage collaboration.
	
¬Č Brief learners on the importance of using natural resources responsibly, emphasising 
the difference between renewable (such as solar energy and water) and non-
renewable resources (such as fossil fuels and minerals).

Teaching Module
5
	
¬Č Use visual aids or examples to illustrate how responsible or irresponsible practices 
impact the environment and future generations.
Identifying Responsible Activities 
	
¬Č Assign each group to brainstorm and list activities that promote responsible use 
of natural resources. Examples might include recycling, water conservation, using 
public transport, or planting trees.
	
¬Č Ask learners to write down their ideas on sticky notes or chart paper.
	
¬Č Groups should discuss how these activities benefit the environment and support 
sustainability.
Identifying Irresponsible Activities 
	
¬Č Groups then identify and list activities that represent irresponsible use of natural 
resources, such as littering, overfishing, deforestation, or excessive fossil fuel 
consumption.
	
¬Č Encourage learners to analyze why these activities are harmful and their long-term 
impacts.
Discussion
	
¬Č Groups present their findings and solutions to the class using visual aids.
	
¬Č Summarize the key takeaways, highlighting responsible practices learners can 
apply in their daily lives.
Outdoor Set up:
Observation Walk: 
	
¬Č Select areas within the school premises or the local environment that showcase 
examples of resource use (e.g., water taps, gardens, energy sources, waste disposal 
sites). 
	
¬Č Groups explore the school or local environment and document examples of 
responsible (e.g. water conservation, recycling) and irresponsible practices (e.g., 
littering, deforestation).
	
¬Č Encourage learners to engage with the environment actively, noticing patterns and 
asking questions such as ‚ÄúHow is this resource being used, and what impact does 
it have?‚ÄĚ
At-Risk Resources 
	
¬Č Groups identify specific resources that are at risk due to irresponsible practices 
(e.g., polluted water sources, eroded soil).
	
¬Č Learners discuss the potential consequences of continued misuse, connecting their 
observations to larger environmental challenges.
	
¬Č Each group brainstorms practical solutions to mitigate irresponsible practices and 
protect at-risk resources. Examples might include planting trees, educating peers 
on resource conservation, or setting up recycling stations. 
Discussion
	
¬Č Groups present their findings, risks identified, and proposed solutions to the class.
	
¬Č Encourage collaboration to refine ideas further.

Teaching Module

Content Tips
	
¬Č Utilizing renewable and non-renewable resources refers to the process of 
extracting, managing, and using these natural resources to meet human needs while 
considering their availability and the impact of their use on the environment and 
future generations.
	
¬Č It involves harnessing them for energy, materials, or other purposes in a way that 
maintains their ability to regenerate. For instance: 
ÔÉľ Generate electricity using water, solar, wind, geothermal and converting organic 
materials like wood, crop and animal waste, or algae.  
ÔÉľ Materials such as wood for construction, minerals for manufacturing and 
processing purposes and fossil fuels for energy production. 
ÔÉľ Other materials such as clay, sand and gravel are used for construction, building 
and other applications. 
	
¬Č Responsible use of renewable resources means; 
ÔÉľ Investing in clean technologies to optimize renewable resource use; this involves 
employing advanced systems, processes, and innovations that minimize 
environmental impact, enhance efficiency, and promote sustainability. Using 
clean technologies not only harnesses renewable resources efficiently but also 
minimizes ecological damage, supports economic development, and builds 
resilience against climate change.
ÔÉľ Avoid over-exploitation, such as over extraction of minerals, excessive logging 
or water diversion.
ÔÉľ Minimize environmental impacts, such as habitat disruption or pollution.
ÔÉľ Promote resource conservation and restoration.
	
¬Č Responsible use of non-renewable resources means; 
ÔÉľ Minimizing consumption to ensure that they last long.
ÔÉľ Maximize efficiency to reduce waste and extend the lifespan of reserves.
ÔÉľ Implement recycling programs to recover and reuse materials.
ÔÉľ Transition to alternative, renewable energy sources to reduce reliance on non-
renewable natural resources.
ÔÉľ Mitigate environmental damage through stricter regulations and remediation 
efforts.
	
¬Č Irresponsible use of renewable and non-renewable resources
ÔÉľ Cutting down of trees for various purposes without replacing them
ÔÉľ Overfishing and use of bad methods of fishing
ÔÉľ Overgrazing of some areas
ÔÉľ Tilling along river banks
Importance of Responsible Utilization of the Resources
ÔÉľ Minimize pollution, habitat destruction, and biodiversity loss.
ÔÉľ Ensure resources are available for future generations.
ÔÉľ Avoid resource scarcity that could drive up costs or create conflicts.
ÔÉľ Reduce greenhouse gas emissions by prioritizing renewable over non-renewable 
resources.
	
¬Č By utilizing resources wisely and balancing consumption with conservation, 
society can meet its current needs while protecting the planet for the future.
Teaching and Learning Materials
	
¬Č Posters and Infographics 
	
¬Č Videos and Documentaries

7

Expected Standard
	
¬Č Renewable and non-renewable resources used responsibly. 
Assessment
	
¬Č Learners to carry out a simple research on the use of renewable and non-renewable

¬Č Practice clean technologies, conservation and restoration methodologies in utilizing

1.1.2. SUB-TOPIC 2: POPULATION AND NATURAL RESOURCES
Introduction
The relationship between population and natural resources is a critical element in understanding 
the dynamics of human development and environmental sustainability. As the Zambian population 
continues to grow, the demand for essential resources such as water, land, energy, and minerals 
increases exponentially. Natural resources are the foundation of human survival and economic 
activity, providing the raw materials for food, shelter, industry, and energy. However, these 
resources are finite, and their overexploitation can lead to environmental degradation, scarcity, 
and conflict.
Population growth amplifies pressure on ecosystems, often leading to unsustainable practices 
in agriculture, mining, deforestation, and energy consumption. In densely populated regions, 
the strain on natural resources can outpace the Earth‚Äôs capacity to regenerate them, resulting in 
pollution, habitat loss, and a decline in biodiversity. 
Specific competence ‚Äď Learners to:
Analyze the relationship between population and natural resources.
Activity 1
Discussing Population and Natural Resources
Learning Environment
The learning environment would be outdoor or indoor set up.
Indoor Set up:
	
¬Č Briefly introduce the relationship between population and natural resources, 
emphasising concepts such as resource scarcity, sustainability, and overutilization.
	
¬Č Pose a question: ‚ÄúWhat happens when population growth exceeds resource 
availability?‚ÄĚ
	
¬Č Assign each group a specific aspect to discuss (such as water use, energy consumption, 
deforestation, or agricultural land).
	
¬Č Groups will analyse how population affects resource availability in their assigned 
aspect.

8
	
¬Č Learners use sticky notes or charts to record their findings, identifying both 
challenges and opportunities.
	
¬Č Groups present their findings to the class.
	
¬Č Encourage peer questions and help refine ideas further.
Outdoor Set up:
Preparation
	
¬Č Select an outdoor location where learners can observe natural surroundings, 
human activity, and environmental impacts (e.g., school grounds, nearby park, or 
community area)
	
¬Č Divide learners into small groups of 4‚Äď6 members.
	
¬Č Briefly explain the activity, focusing on how population growth and human activities 
can impact the environment (e.g., deforestation, pollution, overuse of resources).
	
¬Č Provide examples of what to look for, such as litter, damaged vegetation, or evidence 
of resource use.
Field Observation
	
¬Č Each group explores the outdoor area and identifies visible effects of population on 
the environment.
	
¬Č Encourage learners to take notes and make sketches or diagrams to document what 
they observe.
	
¬Č Groups can categorize their findings, such as ‚Äúpollution,‚ÄĚ ‚Äúloss of vegetation,‚ÄĚ or 
‚Äúresource overuse.‚ÄĚ
Discussion
	
¬Č Groups reconvene to discuss their findings.
	
¬Č Learners analyse the causes behind the observed environmental impacts and consider 
how population activities might contribute (e.g., ‚ÄúWe saw litter near the park, which 
might be due to an increase in visitors without proper waste disposal.‚ÄĚ).
Content Tips
	
¬Č The Link between population and resource demand
ÔÉľ Population increase results in high demand for basic necessities such as food, 
water, energy, and living space.
ÔÉľ Larger populations require more infrastructure, transportation, housing, and 
services, leading to pressure on natural resources.
ÔÉľ Rising standards of living (especially in developing nations) increase per capita 
resource consumption.
	
¬Č Overconsumption outpaces natural replenishment, leading to:
ÔÉľ Soil degradation
ÔÉľ Water shortages
ÔÉľ Loss of biodiversity
ÔÉľ Air and water pollution
	
¬Č Environmental degradation from overpopulation can result in:
ÔÉľ Over-cultivation and desertification
ÔÉľ Overfishing and ocean depletion
ÔÉľ Excessive carbon emissions and climate change
	
¬Č Lack of balance in the ecosystem are created when human activity outstrips the 
environment‚Äôs capacity to regenerate.

Form 1 Term 2
Teaching Module
9
	
¬Č Impacts of overpopulation could be addressed through technological advancements 
such as:
ÔÉľ Renewable energy (solar, wind)
ÔÉľ Water purification and recycling
ÔÉľ Climate-SMART agriculture practices such as crop rotation, agroforestry, 
mulching etc and sustainable agriculture methods.
	
¬Č To manage the population-resource relationship;
ÔÉľ Promote education and family planning to slow population growth.
ÔÉľ Encourage sustainable development and green technologies.
ÔÉľ Implement environmental laws and international agreements (e.g., Paris 
Agreement).
ÔÉľ Shift to circular economies where resources are reused and recycled.
Teaching and Learning Materials
	
¬Č Posters and Infographics on The 3Rs (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle)
	
¬Č Charts and Diagrams showing population and natural resources
	
¬Č Videos and Documentaries on population and natural resources
Assessment
Ask learners to:
ÔÉľ  suggest ways on how to reduce the pressure of population on natural resources.
ÔÉľ debate different viewpoints on managing population and natural resources.
ÔÉľ role play: ‚ÄúA village meeting discussing a water shortage due to increased 
population.‚ÄĚ
ÔÉľ debate on ‚ÄúTechnology vs. Population Control-Which is the better solution for 
managing
ÔÉľ resources?‚ÄĚ
Activity 2
Analysing the relationship between population and natural resources
Learning Environment
The learning activity would be indoor or outdoor set up.
Indoor set up:
	
¬Č Learners in groups discuss how people relate with the natural resources.
	
¬Č Discuss the impact of large population on the natural resources
Outdoor set up:
	
¬Č Learners to visit the local environment and observe how people are relating with the 
natural resources. 
	
¬Č Learners assess some human activities that may be a threat to the natural resources.
Content Tips
	
¬Č The relationship between population and natural resources is dynamic and complex, 
as population growth and resource consumption are interdependent. 
	
¬Č As the population grows, the demand for natural resources increases. This includes 
resources for:

Geography
Teaching Module
10
-	
Food: The demand for agricultural land, water and raising livestock increases 
while the land is limited.
-	
Energy: The demand for energy such as biomass, hydroelectricity for heating and 
transportation leads to eventual issues such as pollution.
-	
Water: The demand for freshwater for domestic use and irrigation accelerates as 
the population increases.
-	
Raw materials: There is also high demand for minerals, metals, and timber for 
construction, manufacturing, and infrastructure.
	
¬Č As cities expand, there is an increase in resource demand for construction, water and 
energy supply.
	
¬Č As populations exceed carrying capacities, competition for resources intensifies, 
potentially leading to conflicts, higher costs, and social inequality.
	
¬Č Population growth accelerates depletion and creating scarcity of natural resources.
	
¬Č The relationship between population and natural resources is reciprocal. A growing 
population increases resource demand, while resource availability influences 
population well-being and growth patterns. 
	
¬Č Sustainable practices, technological innovation, and equitable resource distribution 
are critical to maintaining this balance and ensuring the well-being of both people 
and the planet. 
Teaching and Learning Materials
	
¬Č Charts and Graphs illustrating population growth trends, resource consumption and 
deforestation rates.
	
¬Č Documentaries and videos showing real-life examples of resource depletion and 
conservation efforts in Zambia.
	
¬Č Posters displaying information on sustainable resource use and conservation.
	
¬Č Online Resources: Use websites and digital platforms to access data on Zambia‚Äôs 
population and natural resources.

2.1.1. SUB ‚ÄďTOPIC 1: POPULATION COMPOSITION
Introduction
Population composition refers to the various characteristics that make a population, such as age, 
gender, density and ethnicity. The other elements of population composition include distribution 
and density, wealth, education, marital status, fertility, and mortality. Population structure (also 
referred to as population composition) is the distribution of different social groups across a 
nation or area. There are many various aspects to learn about when studying population structure, 
such as: the meaning of population structure, the characteristics of the structure, the factors 
impacting these characteristics, how to measure it, and the importance of the structure for a 
country. Population structure is the breakdown of different groups of people in an area. It is 
important as it affects the area itself. Population pyramids vary in shape from country to country, 
depending on their level of economic development.
Specific Competence(s) ‚Äď Learners to: 
Demonstrate understanding of population composition.

(e.g., how population growth affects resources).

Content Tips
	
¬Č Population Density: It is the number of people living per square kilometer in an area. 
Population density may be high or low.
	
¬Č Population Distribution: It is the way the population is spread in a given area or 
space. Some places are sparsely, moderately or densely populated.
	
¬Č Population Structure: It is made up of different age groups and sex. Population 
structure is shown using population pyramids.
	
¬Č Birth Rate: It is the number of babies born live in every 1000 people in a population 
in a year. 
	
¬Č Crude Birth rate: is the total number of babies born per 1000 people in a population 
within a given period of time expressed as a percentage. Example: if 40 births occur, 
this means 40/1000 √ó 100 = 4%.  
	
¬Č Death Rate: It is the number of deaths happening in every 1000 people of a population. 
	
¬Č Crude Death rate; It is the number of death per 1000 people in an area over a given 
period of time expressed as a percentage. This is also known as mortality rate. 
Example: if 50 deaths occurred, this means 50/1000 √ó 100 = 5%.  
	
¬Č Migration: This is the movement of people from one place to another for settlement. It 
could be within the country (internal) or between countries (international). Migration 
can either lead to a decrease or increase in population of an area or country.

2.1.3. SUB ‚Äď TOPIC 3: RAPID POPULATION GROWTH IN ZAMBIA
Introduction
Zambia has experienced significant population growth over the past few decades, driven by 
factors such as high birth rates, declining death rates, and immigration. This rapid increase in 
population presents both opportunities and challenges, including pressure on social services, 
infrastructure, and natural resources. Understanding the causes of this growth is essential for 
planning sustainable development and ensuring a better quality of life for future generations.

Content Tips
Rapid population growth is caused by a number of factors such as;
	
¬Č High Birth Rates: Zambia has a high Total Fertility Rate (TFR) of 4.7 children per 
woman as 2022 census, contributing to rapid population growth.
	
¬Č Low Birth Rates: The number of people dying is much lower than those that are born
	
¬Č Early Marriages: Early marriages and childbearing are common in Zambia, leading 
to a higher number of births.
	
¬Č Limited Access to Family Planning: Many Zambians, particularly in rural areas,

¬Č Low Literacy Rates: Low literacy rates, especially among women, contribute to a 
lack of understanding about family planning and reproductive health.
	
¬Č Limited Access to Schools: Many Zambian children, particularly girls, do not have

2.1.4. SUB-TOPIC 4: MIGRATION
Introduction
Migration refers to the movement of people from one place to another, often in search of better 
living conditions, employment opportunities, or safety. In Zambia, migration occurs both 
within the country (internal migration) and across national borders (international migration). 
Internal migration includes rural-to-urban movement, seasonal migration for agricultural work, 
and relocation due to economic factors. Understanding migration helps in analyzing its causes, 
effects, and potential solutions to related challenges.
Specific Competence (s) - learners to:
Apply knowledge of migration in aspects of daily life
 
Activity 1
Discussing Concepts of Migration (migration, immigration, emigration) internal 
(rural-urban, seasonal and international migrations‚Ä¶)
Learning Environment
The learning environment for this activity would be indoor set up
Indoor Set up:
	
¬Č Learners in groups discuss concepts on migration. 
	
¬Č Watching videos or presentations on different types of migration.
	
¬Č Creating a concept map showing types of migration (internal vs. international).
Content Tips
	
¬Č Migration: the movement of people from one place to another permanently of 
temporal.
	
¬Č Immigration: when people enter a new country to live permanently.
	
¬Č Emigration: when people leave their home country to settle elsewhere. 
	
¬Č Internal migration: movement of people within a country.
	
¬Č Rural-Urban migration: people moving from villages to cities for better opportunities.
	
¬Č Urban-Rural migration: people moving from urban areas to rural for various reasons.
	
¬Č International migration: movement across national borders, often for employment 
or education.
	
¬Č Circular migration: temporary movement of people between two or more locations 
often for work or family reasons.
	
¬Č Seasonal migration: is the temporary movement of people from one region to another 
in response to changes in season, weather patterns or agricultural cycles.
	
¬Č Voluntary migration: migration that occurs by choice often for economic or

educational reasons.
	
¬Č Forced migration: migration that occur due to coercion, conflict or persecution.
	
¬Č Asylum seeker: a person who seeks protection from another country due to 
persecution or conflicts.

Content Tips
There are many causes of migration. The causes are divided into push and pull factors.
	
¬Č Push factors are those things that force people to migrate while pull factors are those 
that attract people to settle in different areas. 
	
¬Č Employment Opportunities: People move to areas with better job prospects, such as 
cities or foreign countries.
	
¬Č Access to Social Services: Migration occurs when people seek better healthcare, 
education, and housing.
	
¬Č Recreational and Urban Facilities: Cities attract migrants with entertainment centers, 
modern infrastructure, and advanced services.
	
¬Č Conflicts and Disputes: Wars, political instability, and ethnic conflicts force people 
to migrate for safety.
	
¬Č Natural Disasters: Floods, droughts, and other disasters push people to safer areas.
	
¬Č Food Insecurity: When agricultural production is low, people migrate to regions

Summary 
Understanding of Population composition
	
¬Č Zambia‚Äôs population composition is characterized by a youthful age structure with 
almost a balanced sex ratio and majority of people living in rural areas.
	
¬Č While the population is predominantly of African descent, there are also minority 
racial and ethnic groups, including people from Europe, Asia, and mixed heritage 
particularly in urban areas.
	
¬Č These diverse groups contribute to the country‚Äôs cultural and economic landscape. 
The composition presents both opportunities and challenges such as need for 
increased investment in Education, health care and employment to support the 
growing young population.
	
¬Č Understating these demographic patterns such as age, gender and ethnic diversity is 
essential for effective national planning and sustainable development in the country.

Geography

34
Analyse Population distribution
	
¬Č Zambia‚Äôs population is unevenly distributed across its ten (10) Provinces. It has 
sparsely, moderately and densely populated areas. 
	
¬Č The country‚Äôs population distribution is influenced by various factors such as; 
climate, relief, soils, rainfall distribution, economy activities, Government policies, 
transport etc. 
	
¬Č Zambia has experienced significant population growth due to factors such as high 
birth rates, declining death rates, early marriages, and immigration. 
	
¬Č Understanding population distribution is crucial for effective planning, resource 
allocation and decision making in various sectors, such as healthcare, education and 
infrastructure development.
Challenges of Rapid population growth
	
¬Č Zambia‚Äôs rapid population growth increase has led to both challenges and 
opportunities.
	
¬Č The country‚Äôs rapid population growth has been caused by high birth rates, early 
marriage, declining death rates, better health care services, education etc
	
¬Č Negative effects of rapid population growth include unemployment, overcrowding, 
increased crime rates, and pressure on social services like healthcare and education. 
	
¬Č However, population growth also brings benefits, such as a larger workforce, better 
utilization of natural resources, and improved security. 
	
¬Č Managing these challenges through effective policies, waste management, resource 
conservation, and public awareness is crucial for sustainable development.
Knowledge of Migration in aspects of daily life
	
¬Č Knowledge of migration in aspects of daily life terms such as immigration, 
emigration, and migration are fundamental concepts in people‚Äôs lives.
	
¬Č By exploring these concepts, learners gain an understanding of the different types of 
movement people experience, both within Zambia and across borders.
	
¬Č Migration is caused by push and pull factors influencing due to factors such as 
economic reasons, safety concerns, or the search for better living conditions.
	
¬Č Migration have both positive and negative effects on the destination area and 
the place of origin are both such as cultural diversity, a growing labor force, and 
economic growth, while negative effects involve pressure on resources and housing. 
Migration on the place of origin can reduce unemployment but also lead to labor 
shortages and brain drain. 
	
¬Č Migration further affects the workforce, cultural and societal changes, and 
environmental conditions resulting in a diverse labor force, cultural exchange, and 
societal tensions, while also influencing environmental changes like urbanization 
and deforestation.
